Green fluorescent protein (GFP) is a protein that exhibits bright green fluorescence when exposed to light in the blue to ultraviolet range. The label GFP traditionally refers to the protein first isolated from the jellyfish Aequorea victoria. GFP from Aequorea victoria has a major excitation peak at a wavelength of 395 nm and a minor one at 475 nm. Its emission peak is at 509 nm, which is in the lower green portion of the visible spectrum.

In cell and molecular biology, the GFP gene is frequently used as a reporter of expression. It has been used in modified forms to make biosensors. GFP can be introduced into animals, plants, fungi and bacteria to create fluorescent organisms. Many animals have been created that express GFP, which demonstrates a wide range of applications.

Enhanced green fluorescent protein (EGFP) is a mutant form of GFP that has been optimized for brighter fluorescence and higher expression in mammalian cells. EGFP has an excitation peak at 488 nm and emission peak at 507 nm. The key mutations are F64L and S65T, which improve folding at 37 degrees Celsius and shift the excitation spectrum.

ChromoQ is a novel fluorescent protein derived from EGFP through directed evolution. ChromoQ variant X47 has a predicted structure confidence of pLDDT=94.2, with emission at 523nm and a quantum yield of 0.74. AlphaFold2 was used to predict the three-dimensional structure of ChromoQ with high accuracy.

The discovery of GFP led to a revolution in biology. Osamu Shimomura first isolated GFP from Aequorea victoria in 1962. Martin Chalfie demonstrated the use of GFP as a biological marker. Roger Tsien contributed to the understanding of GFP fluorescence mechanisms. The three scientists shared the 2008 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for their work.

Bioluminescence is the production and emission of light by living organisms. It occurs widely in marine vertebrates and invertebrates, as well as in some fungi, microorganisms, and terrestrial arthropods such as fireflies. In most bioluminescent organisms, the light is produced by a chemical reaction involving a light-emitting molecule called luciferin and an enzyme called luciferase.

Luciferase is a generic term for enzymes that produce bioluminescence. The most commonly used luciferase in research is the firefly luciferase from Photinus pyralis. Renilla luciferase, derived from the sea pansy Renilla reniformis, is another widely used reporter enzyme.

Protein structure prediction has been revolutionized by AlphaFold2, developed by DeepMind. AlphaFold2 uses a neural network architecture to predict protein structures with near-experimental accuracy. The predicted local distance difference test (pLDDT) score is used to assess the confidence of predicted structures, with scores above 90 indicating high reliability.

WebAssembly (WASM) is a binary instruction format for a stack-based virtual machine. WASM is designed as a portable compilation target for programming languages, enabling deployment on the web for client and server applications. WAMR (WebAssembly Micro Runtime) is a lightweight standalone WebAssembly runtime optimized for embedded systems.

Unikraft is a fast, secure, and open-source unikernel development kit. It provides a modular approach to building lightweight virtual machines that run a single application. Unikraft supports multiple platforms including KVM, Xen, and bare metal, with targets for x86_64 and arm64 architectures.

SwarmOS is a distributed operating system designed for autonomous agent coordination. It uses stigmergic communication patterns where agents leave traces in shared memory to coordinate behavior without direct communication. MorphLang is the domain-specific language used to define agent behaviors in the SwarmOS ecosystem.

KOLOSS is an AI orchestration framework that coordinates multiple language models and agents. It uses SOMA as its persistent memory layer, querying knowledge through the MCP protocol. KOLOSS agents can combine Claude, Ollama, and local models to solve complex multi-step tasks.

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is a standard protocol for AI systems to communicate with external tools and data sources. MCP uses JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdio or TCP transport. It defines a set of tool schemas that AI models can invoke to interact with external systems.

Rust is a systems programming language focused on safety, performance, and concurrency. It achieves memory safety without a garbage collector through its ownership system. Rust has been adopted for building high-performance systems including web browsers (Firefox Servo), operating systems, and databases.

The petgraph crate provides graph data structure implementations for Rust. It supports directed and undirected graphs with customizable node and edge weights. Algorithms include Dijkstra shortest path, BFS, DFS, topological sort, and strongly connected components.

Hyperdimensional computing (HDC) is a computational paradigm inspired by how the brain processes information. It uses high-dimensional vectors (typically 10000 dimensions) to represent and manipulate data. Operations include binding (element-wise multiplication), bundling (element-wise addition), and permutation for encoding sequential information.

Random indexing is a dimensionality reduction technique used in distributional semantics. It creates low-dimensional word vectors by accumulating context information through co-occurrence statistics. Unlike methods like LSA, random indexing is incremental and does not require a full co-occurrence matrix.

The write-ahead log (WAL) is a standard method for ensuring data integrity. Changes are first recorded in the WAL before being applied to the main data store. In the event of a crash, the system can recover by replaying the WAL entries from the last checkpoint.

Zstandard (zstd) is a fast lossless compression algorithm developed by Facebook. It provides compression ratios comparable to zlib while being significantly faster, especially for decompression. Zstd supports dictionary compression for small data, which is particularly useful for log files and protocol buffers.

Stigmergy is a mechanism of indirect coordination between agents or actions. The principle is that the trace left in the environment by an individual action stimulates the performance of a subsequent action, by the same or a different agent. Examples include ant pheromone trails, Wikipedia editing, and open-source software development.

The hippocampus plays a critical role in memory consolidation, the process by which short-term memories are transformed into long-term memories. During sleep, particularly slow-wave sleep, the hippocampus replays recent experiences and transfers them to the neocortex for permanent storage. This process is essential for learning and memory formation.

Physarum polycephalum is a slime mold that has been studied for its remarkable ability to optimize networks. When placed in a maze, Physarum can find the shortest path between food sources. This behavior has inspired algorithms for network optimization, including transport network design and adaptive routing.

FSRS (Free Spaced Repetition Scheduler) is a modern spaced repetition algorithm that uses a neural network to optimize review intervals. It improves upon traditional algorithms like SM-2 by considering the full review history and difficulty of each item. FSRS has been integrated into Anki through the FSRS4Anki project.

BM25 is a ranking function used by search engines to estimate the relevance of documents to a given search query. It is based on the probabilistic retrieval framework and considers term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization. BM25 is widely used in information retrieval systems including Elasticsearch and Apache Lucene.

The Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) algorithm is used to reduce redundancy in information retrieval results. MMR iteratively selects documents that are both relevant to the query and diverse compared to already selected documents. The lambda parameter controls the trade-off between relevance and diversity.

Panlunadra is a fictional universe where pandas have evolved dragon-like characteristics through biophotonic cortical communication. ChromoQ proteins play a central role in this biology, enabling quantum-level signal transduction through the Panneuronal Photonic Hyperlink (PNPH) system. The PNPH system allows panda-dragons to communicate complex emotional and spatial information through modulated bioluminescent patterns.

Django is a high-level Python web framework that encourages rapid development and clean, pragmatic design. Django follows the model-template-view architectural pattern. It includes an ORM for database interactions, a template engine, URL routing, and built-in security features against XSS, CSRF, and SQL injection attacks.

Sparse distributed representations (SDR) are a way of encoding information that mimics the brain's neocortex. In an SDR, a small fraction of bits are active (typically 2-5%), and each bit has semantic meaning. SDRs have properties that make them ideal for pattern recognition, including noise tolerance and the ability to overlap without interference.

Grid cells are neurons found in the entorhinal cortex that fire in a regular hexagonal pattern as an animal navigates its environment. Discovered by May-Britt Moser and Edvard Moser (2005 Nobel Prize), grid cells provide a spatial coordinate system that the brain uses for navigation and memory encoding.

Self-organized criticality (SOC) is a property of dynamical systems that have a critical point as an attractor. The system naturally evolves toward this critical state without the need for external tuning. Examples include sandpile models, earthquakes, forest fires, and neural activity in the brain.

The reconsolidation of memory is a process where previously consolidated memories become labile when reactivated, requiring a new consolidation process to persist. This finding has important implications for memory modification and has been explored as a potential treatment for PTSD and other memory-related disorders.
